attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman recently returned from North America presented to Emergency Department with calf pain and acute shortness of breath. She has no known past medical conditions, and is otherwise well. She was investigated with a computer tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA) which demonstrated several intra-arterial thrombi in the interlobular pulmonary tree. She was treated with enoxaparin 80mg (1 mg/kg) for two days before commencing rivaroxaban 15mg BD.

On her sixth day of admission, she again develops shortness of breath, fever and chest pain. She has a repeat set of investigations showing platelet count of 50x10^9/L (previously 350; 150 – 450), white cell count 15 x109 /L (4-11), haemoglobin 120g/L (125-160), negative blood cultures and a troponin of 24 (<40). Her CTPA shows new thrombi within the lung vasculature. Her electrocardiogram demonstrates sinus tachycardia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is an example of heparin induced thrombocytopenia thrombosis syndrome (HITTS). This is an immune-mediated reaction to the PF4 part of the heparin molecule. This can result in platelets clumping together (dropping the level) which can result in thrombosis and several complications including DVT, PE, myocardial infarction, stroke and skin necrosis. It can occur with any heparin-based medication but is a greater risk with unfractionated heparin and in larger, therapeutic doses.

It commonly presents 5-10 days post administration of heparin and can present as with a myriad of symptoms/complications including venous thrombosis, arterial thrombosis, skin necrosis, and acute systemic reactions mimicking PE or anaphylaxis. It carries a high risk of mortality and needs to be treated immediately.

Diagnosis and assessment is via the 4T score which had 4 elements.

  1. Thrombocytopenia (Pt count fall >50% = 2 points, fall 30-50% = 1)

  2. Timing of platelet count (within 5-10 days of exposure = 2 points, after 10 days or fall within a day, anytime within 100days of heparin = 1 point)

  3. Thrombosis (new thrombosis, skin necrosis or systemic reaction = 2 points; suspected, progressive or recurrent thrombosis = 1 point)

  4. Other cause for thrombocytopenia (none = 2 points, possible causes = 1 point, definite other cause = 0 points)

A low probability of HITTS is <3 points, a high probability is 6-8 points. Our patient scores at least 6 points pending investigation for other causes of thrombocytopenia and thus is very likely to have HITTS. Further testing such as immunoassays can be used to definitively diagnosis the condition.

The key to treatment is immediate cessation of heparin, and change to a non-heparin anticoagulant to ensure the patient is being adequately treated for any thrombosis. Fondaparinux is a direct factor 10 inhibitor and is commonly used in cases of HITTS.

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is an autoimmune condition caused by antiphospholipid antibodies. APS is often associated with systemic lupus erythematous (SLE), but may be idiopathic or secondary to infections, medications or malignancies. APS results in both thrombi and thrombocytopenia, however in this case, the recurrent pulmonary emboli after anticoagulation is more in keeping with HITTS.

Pseudothrombocytopaenia is an in-vitro sampling problem often mediated by the EDTA anticoagulant, whereby platelet agglutination results in a false low platelet count in automated cell counting. This option is unlikely, given the patient is acutely symptomatic with confirmed pulmonary emboli.

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura is typically associated with a haemolytic anaemia which is not present in this case nor is there any mention of cutaneous purpura.

Septicaemia with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) commonly occurs in the setting of severe, systemic illness presenting with both bleeding and clotting abnormalities which is not presented here. Sepsis itself can increase the turnover of platelets, resulting in a transient mild-moderate thrombocytopaenia. DIC actively consumed platelets and coagulation factors through the formation of microthrombi in blood vessels. Normal blood cultures make septicaemia less likely, although not impossible.
learningPoints: Heparin induced thrombocytopenia thrombosis syndrome (HITTS) commonly presents 5-10 days post administration of heparin
HITTS can present as with a myriad of symptoms/complications including venous thrombosis, arterial thrombosis, skin necrosis, and acute systemic reactions mimicking PE or anaphylaxis.
references: Joseph J, Rabbolini D, Enjeti AK, Favaloro E, Kopp MC, et al. Diagnosis and management of heparin‐induced thrombocytopenia: a consensus statement from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand HIT Writing Group. Med J Aust. 2019;210:11. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31155728/
Nickson C. HITTS. In: Life in the Fast Lane [online]; 2020. Accessed 2021 Jan 26. Available at: https://litfl.com/heparin-induced-thrombotic-thrombocytopaenia-syndrome-hitts-or-hit/.
